title: Energy and climate policy
gist: A Bruegel topic-landing page aggregating its 2025-26 energy and climate research agenda and recent outputs; the signal is in the teaser headlines: a 2026 European energy/oil supply shock tied to the Iran conflict, EU state-aid responses, China clean-tech overcapacity, and EU carbon-border (CBAM) spillovers. (This is an index page, not a single article — extraction is limited to its intro and listed teaser blurbs.)

insights.claim: Europe faces a possible oil supply crunch in 2026: with falling commercial and emergency oil reserves, an oil price shock could become a volume (availability) crisis Europe must prepare for.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: A shift from price to volume risk in European oil implies rationing/supply-security trades, not just spread positioning — monitor EU reserve levels.
insights.quote: With falling commercial and emergency oil reserves, Europe’s oil price shock could soon become a volume crisis – a scenario for which it must prepare
insights.locator: Analysis — Europe must prepare for a possible oil supply crunch

insights.claim: A 2026 Iran conflict is driving an energy shock affecting EU gas prices and availability, prompting an EU temporary state-aid framework that Bruegel warns should not become permanent.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: Temporary state aid tends to ratchet; watch for permanent EU energy subsidies that distort the single market and fiscal positions.
insights.quote: The latest EU temporary state-aid framework is needed to shield industries and consumers from short-term costs, but should not become more permanent
insights.locator: First Glance — The Iran energy shock is a test of European discipline on state aid

insights.claim: The US has largely replaced Russia as an energy supplier to the EU, but Bruegel argues dependence on fossil fuels, not dependence on the United States specifically, is Europe's real vulnerability.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: Reframes EU energy-security risk away from supplier identity toward fossil exposure — supportive of the decarbonisation/transition case over supplier diversification alone.
insights.quote: Dependence on fossil fuels, not on the United States, is Europe’s worry
insights.locator: Analysis — Dependence on fossil fuels, not on the United States

themes.theme: Clean-tech industrial policy and carbon pricing

insights.claim: China's green industrial strategy has outpaced demand, leaving renewables firms under strain, with grid expansion framed as the key test of market rebalancing.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: Confirms the China clean-energy overcapacity theme as a recurring Bruegel focus; grid buildout is the watched rebalancing mechanism.
insights.quote: China’s green industrial strategy has outpaced demand, leaving renewables firms under strain and grid expansion as the key test of market rebalancing
insights.locator: Working paper — green infrastructure / China overcapacity

insights.claim: The EU carbon border adjustment mechanism (CBAM) is nudging trade-exposed partners toward carbon pricing, but the spillovers are concentrated in high and upper-middle-income countries.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: CBAM is exporting carbon pricing unevenly; lower-income trade partners lag, a fault line for trade tension and EU import costs.
insights.quote: EU CBAM is nudging trade-exposed partners towards carbon pricing, but spillovers are concentrated in high and upper middle-income countries
insights.locator: Working paper — Carbon pricing beyond borders
